does: This paper proposes Neuron Auctions, a mechanism for embedding advertisements into large language model outputs by conducting auctions in the model's internal representation space rather than the surface text. The authors use mechanistic interpretability to identify brand-specific neurons in feed-forward networks and show that different brands activate in approximately orthogonal subspaces. They then design auction mechanisms where the auctionable goods are continuous, disentangled intervention budgets—specifically neuron counts and amplification factors—that can be independently controlled to influence which brands appear in generated text. The approach allows rigorous mechanism design while preserving semantic coherence of LLM outputs, addressing the tension between advertiser payoffs, platform revenue, and user experience in generative advertising.
connects: This work extends recent research on mechanistic interpretability of neural networks, building on prior findings that internal network components encode interpretable features and can be surgically modified. It combines mechanistic interpretability insights with classical algorithmic mechanism design, applying auction theory to a novel domain. The paper responds to limitations of earlier advertising injection methods like prompt injection and fixed-position slots, which lack parametric control and degrade output quality. It also engages with the broader literature on LLM monetization strategies and the challenge of maintaining user experience while enabling platform revenues, topics increasingly relevant as conversational AI systems deploy at scale.
enables: This framework opens several future research directions. It enables more sophisticated auction designs tailored to the advertiser-platform-user trilemma, potentially incorporating user preferences directly into mechanism design. The approach of treating internal representations as auctionable resources could extend beyond advertising to other applications requiring parametric control over model behavior—such as personalization, safety interventions, or content moderation. The work also suggests deeper investigation into the linear structure and orthogonality properties of neuron activations across multiple domains, potentially revealing which other concepts can be independently manipulated at scale. Finally, it raises questions about scalability and robustness of mechanistic interventions in larger models, and whether similar orthogonal structure holds for different architectures or model scales.
